All right, you're back and it's time now to get specific with your third loop. So what I'm going to do here is slow things down for you a bit and take you one by one through each of this third loop's five steps, starting with step number one, which is what? Are you going to rebut the new objection? It's one. Two. Are you going to loop back into presentation mode? Three. Are you going to deflect this objection? Or four. Are you going to ratchet up their pain? What's the first thing you're going to do here? Answer right now. Exactly. As it is with all loops beyond your first loop where you actually deflected the first objection, your first step is always going to be rebutting the objection your prospect hit you with. That's step number one here. No, no, no, you're not going to ratchet up the pain threshold. Not just yet. That's coming. But here's the deal. As it is with all loops, step number one, except for that first loop where you deflect, step number one here is going to be to rebut the objection your prospect hit you with. No, no, no, you're not going to deflect this objection. Deflection only happens with your first loop. In other words, in response to the first objection you get hit with, you deflect that. If you keep doing that, you'll start sounding evasive, your break report not going to work. As it is with all loops beyond your first loop, the first step here is going to be to rebut the objection your prospect hit you with. No, no, no, not exactly here. A bit of a trick question, I guess. But yeah, that's going to be your next step. But your first step here is always going to be to rebut the objection your prospect hit you with. That's going to be your first step. And then from there, your second step is going to be to insert your pain-increasing pattern, meaning you're going to begin the process now of digging into the pain points that you extracted from Bill during the intelligence gathering phase in the front half of the sale. You get it? In the front half, while you were asking those all-important intelligence gathering questions, you extracted Bill's pain. You found out what those deep worries were at the base of the skull. What's keeping him up at night? Well, that's going to be instrumental now in doing what? Well, let's put up that graphic here of the scale. Remember the folk room? Well, as you start adding on pain right now, that is going to cause this folk room to slip back to center here and give all the positives massive force and take away some of the impact of the negatives. All right? So let me take you through this right now, step by step. I'll do it very slowly. All right? So here we go. Now it starts with your prospect Bill hitting you with that third objection. So let's pretend he said that in truth, it's actually now that he's not liquid. That's what's his real objection now. You get it? In other words, here's what happened. He started off by saying, let me think about it, to which you deflected that objection, you ran your first loop, you went backwards, rebuilt certainty, right? And then you asked for the order now a second time. That's how that loop ended. What happened there? Well, to that, he then hopped to, oh, it sounds really good, but let me call you back. And to that, you then answered that objection head on, right? You couldn't deflect that one. And then you loop back again, increase certainty for the three tens, and then you lowered his action threshold and asked for the order now a third time. So what did he do? He says, oh, you know, it sounds really good. But the truth is now, when you suddenly get a complete, I'm not liquid right now, as if that was his real objection. You get it? Now, here's the deal. From here, what you're going to do is you're going to obviously answer this rebuttal head on. You can't deflect, right? You always answer rebuttals after the first one, right? So I think we have about 30 or 40 plus rebuttals in the back end here in the resource center to show you how to answer, I'm not liquid. So let me just choose one here. The important thing to remember always is that the one you choose has the appropriate context. I'll go through that in a different lesson. So I'd say something along the lines of, you know, Bill, I totally understand you're not liquid right now, empathetic tone. I mean, I didn't expect to be waiting for you with a bag of money by your side. But here's the deal, Bill. Every successful business person has a little bit of money set aside for a special situation. And Bill, this situation is exactly that. In fact, let me ask you this. And now let me ask you, this is what? Well, this is your segue now to digging into whatever Bill's primary pain point is, which again, you'd extracted during the intelligence gathering phase in the front half of the sale. In other words, instead of looping back here, you're doing sort of a modified loop. You're going to actually dig into pain first, which will lower that action threshold dramatically. And then what you'll do is you'll resolve that pain by telling him a story about another prospect in a similar position to him. And that's going to essentially build certainty. I'll get to that in a moment. Okay. So what you're going to do here is take the pain that you know exists and you begin digging into. You amplify that pain by saying, Bill, let me ask you this. What's going to happen if you don't do anything at all? If you don't take action to fix this problem? For example, using a very empathetic tone so you don't break rapport, you would say, Bill, let me ask you a question. You know, given how things have kind of deteriorated over the last 12 months, where do you see yourself in another year of narrative? Where's five years from now? Things that are getting even more intense, more sleepless nights, more worrying. And again, make sure you have a very sympathetic tone here. Not like, where's it going from me, Bill? If you do that, you're done. You'll break rapport. But if you do this right and you took the time to really identify what their pain was and now you reintroduce it here, nine out of 10 times, your prospect will say something like, yeah, well, best, maybe I'll be in the same spot, but I'm kind of worried about this. I'll probably be a lot worse off. And then here's an interesting thing that happens. Human beings have this odd habit. We tend to pick at our own scabs. We self-torture. We flagellate. And if you give the prospect time, he'll just start telling you about how terrible things are going to be and how worried he is. Let them speak and say, uh-huh. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. Ooh. Use your active listening to amplify that. And then make sure that you keep digging in further with follow-up questions, following that logical progression. That's your next step. Dig in with follow-up questions. Step after that. Very simple. Now you're going to tell a story about another client that was just like him and do not lie here. Ethics, ethics, ethics, but find a situation or if you don't have one, use someone else in your company who's been there longer than you. Tell a story about a client who is in the exact or very similar situation to them and how they were able to use your product, good or service to get themselves into a whole different situation. And now they're feeling great. They're back in control. Their family is kind of whatever you're selling. And then from there now, you're going to transition into a soft close. And if possible, if you have a product or service or good that allows you to do one of those step-down sales, meaning to reduce the commitment even more for the first one, I want you to step down now to the minimum that the firm or your company will allow you to buy and you ask him for the order one more times. You can say something along the lines of like, you know, build this and I've been around the block a few thousand times. And I know that things like this, problems like these, they don't typically resolve themselves. You have to take action. So why don't we do this? Since it's our first time working together, why don't we start off at the lowest level here, give you a chance to dip your toe in the water, see how well this works you, give me a shot bill and believe me, if you do even half as well as everyone else in this program, the only problem you'll have is it'll call you six months ago and get you started then. Does that sound fair enough? And now you shut up. Please, please, please. This is where I do not want you to talk through your clothes. In some level, the first person who talks now loses, although this should be a win-win for you and your prospect. Here's the deal. This is the point now in the encounter where you have to make some decisions. You've now been through three solid loops, right? You've done your job. You've lowered the action threshold. You've added on paying. You've essentially picked the lock to their buying strategy, right? Now does that mean everyone's going to buy? No, no way. In fact, there may be people who won't buy. Now there are different reasons for that. One will be they're still not at that highest level of certainty in which case you would have to run additional loops because you could keep running more and more loops here and keep building more certainty, lowering action threshold, adding on more pain. You can do that. And in fact, depending on the industry, you're going to want to. That's one possibility. Another possibility is to end the call now based on what the objection is. They want to think about it, call you back, whatever it might be, and you will get a set up a follow-up call. They want to speak to their wife, set up a follow-up call, and then we're going to go into a whole different level here in another lesson of following up. How do you follow up with people who don't buy on the first go round? That's massive. Very sophisticated and effective follow-up campaign. You get it? Or number three, you could also simply end the call. You get to the point where you're like, you know what? It's diminishing returns. They're really not interested. They really can't afford it, whatever it might be. You get that sense. And by the way, it's a sixth sense. It was that, you know, they're just not going to buy from you. And that's going to happen. And the more you do this and the more proficient you become, you'll start to really get a good idea of who should be followed up with and who should just be weeded out not to waste time. Now, going back to this idea that you can keep running more and more loops, well, the question is how many loops can you run? Good question, right? Well, here's the deal. The number is actually infinite. You keep running loops and keep going back, building more certainty, lowering action threshold, adding up. You can go on and on and on. But that's a subject I want to cover in the next lesson. Go with our follow up campaigns in terms of what we do when we don't want to keep pushing the envelope on one call and when we have a lower sale cycle. So let's stop now. I want you to take a quiz. Very important to lock this stuff in. There's a lot that we went through. And when you come back, we'll discuss what you do with people who still haven't bought right now and how do you really make that decision? It's a sixth sense somewhat, but there is strategy behind that you can start with and you'll get better and better at solve your time, right? So I'll give you sort of the basics of how you make that decision. And from there, we'll delve deeply into follow up campaigns. Get into the point you truly can't close anyone who's closeable. I'll see you there.
